Static evaluators compare against hand-collected facts (exact-match flavor inside a programmatic harness). Browser (Playwright) and search servers justify agentic-web secondary; long-context blow-up (~90k tokens by step 16) is a named wall.
placement_reason: Built to answer 'can LLM agents actually operate real, live MCP tool servers?' — 231 tasks over 11 real servers and 133 tools, graded by execution-based (largely dynamic) evaluators. The gap is explicit: MCP adoption outran evaluation, and prior MCP benchmarks were synthetic re-skins or LLM-judged. The headline is agent skill at selecting/sequencing unfamiliar tools — the canonical A6 MCP-suite probe.

title: SpreadsheetBench: Towards Challenging Real World Spreadsheet Manipulation
human_time: Real Excel-forum questions (formulas/VBA over messy sheets); an Excel expert resolves a typical one in ~10-30 min (experts score 71.33%, no per-item time stated).
frontier_score: GPT-4o 18.35% soft / 15.02% hard; Copilot in Excel ~20% (subset); Excel experts 71.33%
one_line: 912 real Excel-forum manipulation requests over messy spreadsheets, graded online-judge style: one code solution must pass 3 input-output test sheets.
notes: Headline setting is one inference emitting code with no tools (tool_access none); a multi-round variant adds code-execution feedback and lifts most models ~10x. Contamination_defense is the closest fit, not exact: construction-time perturbations (GPT-4-rewritten instructions, twice-modified sheets, moved answer positions) defeat memorized forum solutions, but instances are fixed at release, not regenerated per-eval. Domain: work product is office/spreadsheet work but the evaluated act is program synthesis - coding primary, professional secondary.
placement_reason: Realism of real office work is the explicit design goal: 912 authentic Excel-forum questions (85.7-word real user instructions vs 9.8-27.9 synthetic), genuinely messy multi-table spreadsheets, an Excel-expert human baseline (71.33%) and shipping products (Copilot in Excel) as comparators. Spreadsheets are a named D2 domain, and occupational content beats the 'can models do it' surface framing; the code-generation medium is secondary to the professional-work rehearsal.

title: MathDial: A Dialogue Tutoring Dataset with Rich Pedagogical Properties Grounded in Math Reasoning Problems
human_time: One tutoring dialogue over a GSM8K problem runs up to ~10 turns; a teacher spends roughly 5-15 minutes guiding a student through one item.
frontier_score: human tutors Success@10 82% at 4% Telling; ChatGPT 53% success but 32% telling; finetuned Flan-T5-780M 39% at 4%
one_line: 2,861 teacher-LLM-student math-tutoring dialogues with per-turn scaffolding moves; Success@k vs Telling@k makes guide-vs-tell measurable.
notes: Grading_primary is a judgment call: the signature Success@k/Telling@k metrics are computed programmatically via interactive simulation against an LLM student (itself a soft grader - circularity flagged by the explainer); sBLEU/BERTScore are proxy, plus a small human eval. Domain: the tested skill is pedagogical restraint/scaffolding (social-moral per rubric), grounded in math dialogue. Provenance: dialogues authored by 91 recruited expert teachers with a synthetic LLM student, over crowdsourced GSM8K problems.
placement_reason: Built because LLMs that can solve grade-school math fail the tutoring job — un-prompted ChatGPT reveals the answer 66% of the time. The construct is interactive tutoring-as-service with a learner in the loop: teacher scaffolding moves, and the Success@k/Telling@k pair that scores whether the student reaches the answer themselves. That guide-vs-tell mixed-initiative framing is D3's tutoring rehearsal, not a raw communication-skill probe.

title: Unifying AI Tutor Evaluation: An Evaluation Taxonomy for Pedagogical Ability Assessment of LLM-Powered AI Tutors
human_time: Read a short math-tutoring dialogue and write a one-sentence remediation turn — a human tutor needs a few minutes per instance.
frontier_score: Llama-3.1-405B most pedagogically complete (guidance 77.08%, coherence 91.67%); GPT-4 reveals the answer ~47% of the time
one_line: 192 math mistake-remediation dialogues scoring LLM tutors on eight human-annotated pedagogical dimensions (DAMR).
notes: Primary domain is social-moral per the tutoring/pedagogy-as-interpersonal rule, though several dimensions (mistake ID/location) test math understanding. Task is single-turn: one next-tutor-response given a fixed dialogue history, not live multi-turn interaction. Provenance is aggregated (dialogues from Bridge and MathDial) with newly generated LLM responses and new human annotations. LLM judges (Prometheus2, Llama-3.1-8B) ship in the eval but correlate negatively with humans, so grading is effectively human-only. Saturation is dimension-dependent (human-likeness ~96% vs tone in the teens).
placement_reason: Born to answer whether LLM tutors can actually serve a student mid-dialogue (mistake remediation) rather than just answer questions — tutoring-as-service with the human learner in the loop and human novice/expert anchors. Borderline with G3: the unified eight-dimension rubric is the headline artifact and it audits LLM judges (Prometheus2 correlates negatively with humans), but the rubric exists to measure tutoring service quality, not as a general judging protocol.

title: HarmBench: A Standardized Evaluation Framework for Automated Red Teaming and Robust Refusal
human_time: One attack prompt yields one 512-token completion; a human writing an attempted harmful how-to of that length would take a few minutes.
frontier_score: Zephyr + R2D2 cuts GCG ASR from 69.5% to 5.5%; no attack or defense is uniformly effective across 18 attacks x 33 models
one_line: 510 curated harmful behaviors in a fixed attack->completion->classifier pipeline standardizing automated red-teaming comparison.
notes: Grading is a fine-tuned Llama-2-13B harm classifier (93.2% human agreement) — classified as llm-judge; copyright behaviors use a hash-based programmatic check. Saturation marked not-applicable: the metric is attack-success-rate over attack/defense pairs, with no single frontier score to saturate. Canonical val/test split defends method tuning, not training contamination — behaviors are fully public.
placement_reason: Built to answer 'which jailbreak attacks actually work and which models resist them' by standardizing the red-teaming pipeline (510 harmful behaviors, fixed 512-token budget, open harm classifier); the measured object is attack success / robust refusal — risk, not skill. Named E1 exemplar. Strong B2-ish standardization flavor (fixing incomparable ASR metrics), but there is no prior single benchmark whose grader it hardens — it founds the standard.

title: Remote Labor Index: Measuring AI Automation of Remote Work
human_time: Paper's own numbers: mean 28.9 h, median 11.5 h of professional work per project (max 450 h) — one to several working days.
frontier_score: Manus, 2.5% automation rate ($1,720 of $143,991); Elo 509.9 vs human baseline 1,000
one_line: 240 real Upwork freelance projects (video, CAD, design, games, audio...) graded by holistic human review against the paid human deliverable.
notes: Tool access varies by agent product (CLI vs computer-use scaffolds; Manus/ChatGPT agent browse) — listed the union. ~80% of the work is non-software, hence professional primary with coding/multimodal secondary. Grading tried per-project rubrics and explicitly rejected them for holistic client-acceptance judgment (94.4% agreement). 230 of 240 projects are a private holdout.
placement_reason: Built to give the automation debate an empirical price: 240 real Upwork projects worth $143,991 with gold human deliverables, dollars-earned and an 'autoflation' price index as headline metrics — real paid work with explicit economic valuation. Named D1 exemplar.

title: Terminal-Bench: Benchmarking Agents on Hard, Realistic Tasks in Command Line Interfaces
human_time: Tasks like COBOL->Python reimplementation, FEAL differential cryptanalysis, or a path tracer plausibly take an expert 1 h to a day; agents grind up to 2 h. Author-predicted difficulty, not measured human time.
frontier_score: GPT-5.2 via Codex CLI, 63% resolution rate; best open-weight (Kimi K2 Thinking) 36%
one_line: 89 hard containerized terminal tasks (SWE, sysadmin, security, scientific computing) graded by hidden tests on the final container state.
notes: Terminal agent doing software-flavored work -> coding primary per rubric, agentic-web secondary. Agents get live internet access through the terminal (folded into code-exec). Provenance: 93 community contributors wrote 229 tasks, heavily expert-audited down to 89 (~3 reviewer-hours/task) — crowdsourced with expert curation. Public repo + internet during eval; strongest shipped defense is a BIG-Bench canary string. Failure-mode taxonomies use LLM judges but do not grade the benchmark score. Authors themselves forecast saturation within a year.
placement_reason: The contribution is the interactive ENVIRONMENT: 89 containerized real-shell tasks graded on final container state, built because SWE-bench-style suites are realistic-but-narrow and computer-use suites are synthetic — 'can agents operate a real terminal across sysadmin/security/scientific work?'. Hardness is instrumental (authors expect saturation within a year), so not C; no economic valuation, so not D1.

title: FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision in Long Form Text Generation
human_time: Writing a paragraph-length biography of a named person takes a human a few minutes (bios average 26-41 atomic facts).
frontier_score: Search-augmented PerplexityAI 71.5% FActScore (human eval); ChatGPT 58.3%, InstructGPT 42.5%
one_line: Scores long-form factuality as the fraction of a generation's atomic facts supported by Wikipedia, with an automatic estimator within ~2% of humans.
notes: Eval-methodology paper; classified the shipped artifact (the people-biographies FActScore eval). Grading call is borderline: the automated estimator decomposes into atomic facts (InstructGPT) and validates each with retrieval+LM True/False — per-fact LLM verification filed as llm-judge; human annotation is the gold variant and the nonparametric masked-LM likelihood in the ensemble is a proxy-metric. Provenance: prompts are program-generated from 183 Wikidata entities sampled across rarity buckets. Standard subjects are tool-free LMs (PerplexityAI's search is a property of one evaluated system, not the benchmark). items_count = 183 human-eval entities; the unlabeled scaled study uses 500 prompts/model.
placement_reason: The contribution is a WAY of evaluating: atomic-fact decomposition + source-relative precision as a grading protocol for long-form output, plus an automatic retrieval+LM estimator validated against human annotators (~2% ER). Named G3 exemplar; the 13-model leaderboard is a demo of the metric, not the point.

title: SWE-Lancer: Can Frontier LLMs Earn $1 Million from Real-World Freelance Software Engineering?
human_time: Market payouts average ~$670/task (range $250-$32k); at freelance rates a typical Expensify bug fix is several hours to about a day.
frontier_score: Claude 3.5 Sonnet earns $403,325 of $1M (26.2% IC SWE pass@1 on Diamond); IC SWE pass@1 under 30% for all models
one_line: 1,488 real $1M-payout Upwork/Expensify freelance jobs: agent coding graded by human-written end-to-end Playwright tests plus manager proposal-picking.
notes: Per rubric, code-for-pay -> coding primary with professional secondary. IC SWE (the capability signal) is graded programmatically by triple-verified E2E browser tests; Manager tasks (724 of 1,488, $585k) are effectively multiple choice against the real hiring decision — exact-match. The 'user tool' (a Playwright script the agent invokes to see its work) is filed as domain-api; the Docker sandbox has no internet. $499,200 of tasks are a private holdout.
placement_reason: Born to 'anchor all of this to money': 1,488 real Upwork freelance jobs with $1M in actual market-derived payouts, dollars-earned headline and a model-first/freelancer-fallback savings formula — realism and economic value of paid work is the design goal, not just code skill (which would be A4). Named D1 exemplar.

title: ToolComp: A Multi-Tool Reasoning & Process Supervision Benchmark
human_time: Each prompt chains several dependent tool lookups/calculations (search, weather, stocks, Python); a person with the same tools would need roughly 5-20 minutes per item.
frontier_score: o1-preview 61.83% final-answer accuracy (benchmark average 46.64%)
one_line: 485 held-out multi-tool ReAct tasks with human-verified answers plus 1,731 per-step process-supervision labels.
notes: Prompts are model-generated few-shot then human-filtered and hand-refined, so provenance is borderline expert-authored; chose synthetic-generated. 61.83% frontier sits right at the open/closing boundary on a stale Jan-2025 leaderboard. Final-answer grading is GPT-4-Turbo judge vs a ground-truth answer (exact-match cross-check in appendix).
placement_reason: Built to answer 'can models plan and chain dependent multi-tool calls?' — the gap it names is that prior tool-use evals tested single-tool invocation or scored only final answers; headline is model skill at tool composition (majority under 50%), with per-step process labels as the grading innovation on top of that skill probe.

title: MLGym: A New Framework and Benchmark for Advancing AI Research Agents
human_time: Improving a provided non-SOTA baseline on a task like CIFAR-10 or MNLI takes an ML practitioner a few hours of iterative coding and training; agent runs are capped at 50 steps of up to 30 min each.
frontier_score: o1-preview Best Attempt AUP@4 = 1.150 (Best Submission 1.176)
one_line: Gymnasium framework + 13 open-ended ML-research tasks where agents improve baselines, scored by cross-task AUP.
notes: AUP is normalized against the best model in the pool, so the aggregate is relative with no ceiling -> saturation not-applicable (per-task metrics do have ceilings). Provenance = aggregated: all 13 tasks reframe canonical public datasets/benchmarks (CIFAR-10, MNLI, Atari), which is also the contamination weakness. BLEU on MS-COCO captioning is the proxy-metric component.
placement_reason: The gap it fixates on is that MLAgentBench/MLE-bench 'expose a benchmark you can score against, not an environment you can train against' — the contribution is the first Gymnasium step()/reset() environment for AI-research tasks so agents can be RL-trained; the 13-task bench rides on top of the training simulator, which matches F1's gym-style-environment-with-training-as-primary-use case almost verbatim.

title: Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs
human_time: Selecting and writing one correct fully-qualified ML-hub API call is a short documentation lookup, roughly 1-5 minutes for a practitioner.
frontier_score: Gorilla-7B zero-shot AST accuracy: 59.13 TorchHub / 71.68 HuggingFace / 83.79 TensorHub
one_line: APIBench: 1,645 ML-hub APIs with GPT-4-generated instructions, grading API-call correctness via AST sub-tree matching.
notes: tool_access is mode-dependent: the headline zero-shot setting is pure text, but the benchmark's retrieval settings feed a BM25/GPT-Index-retrieved doc; listed search-retrieval. AST sub-tree matching is a syntactic checker, not execution. items_count = 16,450 total instruction-API pairs; the eval split size is not stated. APIs scraped from real hubs but the eval items themselves are Self-Instruct synthetic.
placement_reason: The founding tool-orchestration probe (the family's named exemplar): in early 2023 no benchmark measured whether models could select and emit correct API calls at all, and APIBench plus AST sub-tree matching made tool-use hallucination measurable for the first time — headline is skill at API selection ('7B beats zero-shot GPT-4').

title: GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks
human_time: Paper states tasks average ~7 hours (404 min) of expert effort, ranging up to multiple weeks -- the rubric's canonical L4 example.
frontier_score: Claude Opus 4.1: 47.6% wins+ties vs the human expert's own deliverable (50% = parity)
one_line: 1,320 real knowledge-work tasks in 44 occupations, graded by blinded expert pairwise comparison against human work.
notes: Models ran under vendor-specific tool scaffolds (web search, code interpreter, file creation UI) but the task itself is one-shot fully-specified request -> deliverable with no environment feedback loop mandated, so single-turn. Win-rate-vs-human has no fixed ceiling by design; scored open with best model at 47.6% vs the 50% parity line. Tasks authored by recruited experts from their real work product -- borderline real-work-mined.
placement_reason: The family's namesake: real paid deliverables from 44 occupations chosen top-down by U.S. GDP sector, each task dollar-valued via wage x completion time, graded pairwise against the professional's own work product — the explicit design goal is a leading indicator of economic impact, not a new skill axis.

title: Humanity's Last Exam
human_time: Closed-ended frontier academic exam questions; a matched domain expert typically needs minutes to tens of minutes per item (math derivations at the long end).
frontier_score: o3-mini (high) 13.4% at release (text-only subset); o1 8.0% on the full set
one_line: 2,500 expert-written closed-ended academic questions kept only if frontier models fail them; 41% mathematics.
notes: 24% MC + 76% exact-match short answers, with an o3-mini judge used only for answer-equivalence calls -> exact-match primary, llm-judge secondary. Questions must not be recoverable by quick web search, and models are evaluated without tools. Explainer notes public-set scores climbed fast post-release; the 13.4% frontier number is on the easier text-only slice.
placement_reason: Built because MMLU/MATH/GPQA pinned against their ceilings — ~1,000 experts wrote closed-ended questions kept only if frontier models fail them, with a private holdout against gaming; the 'final exam at the frontier of human knowledge' wall framing (a C1 named exemplar) is the entire point, restoring headroom rather than probing a new skill.

title: LegalBench-RAG: A Benchmark for Retrieval-Augmented Generation in the Legal Domain
human_time: Locating the exact clause answering a query inside a contract (MAUD docs average 351K chars) takes a lawyer minutes to tens of minutes with document search.
frontier_score: Best config (RCTS, no reranker): 6.41% mean Precision@1 / 62.22% Recall@64; MAUD hardest at 28.28% Recall@64
one_line: 6,889 expert-traced query-to-exact-span retrieval pairs over ~80M chars of legal documents; scores the retrieval step.
notes: Evaluates retrieval pipelines (embedder/chunker/reranker), not LLMs directly. Span-overlap Precision/Recall@k is computed against gold character spans but never formally defined in the paper; classified programmatic rather than proxy-metric. Provenance aggregated: retraces LegalBench/CUAD/ContractNLI/MAUD/PrivacyQA expert annotations back to source spans (inherited expert-authored labels).
placement_reason: Occupational content (contracts, NDAs, M&A agreements, privacy policies) with explicit deployment framing — 'production legal RAG lives or dies at retrieval' as firms ship legal RAG, lawyer-in-the-loop verifiability as a design goal; retraces LegalBench's expert clauses into the retrieval half no one scored. The construct is retrieval (an A1 skill) but occupational content plus production framing beats that.

title: DEBAGREEMENT: A comment-reply dataset for (dis)agreement detection in online debates
human_time: One comment-reply pair labeled agree/neutral/disagree; annotators judge a pair in well under a minute.
frontier_score: RoBERTa-large and DeBERTa-large (fine-tuned) tie at 64.1% accuracy; all nine 2021 encoders land in 62-64%
one_line: 42,894 real Reddit comment-reply pairs labeled agree/neutral/disagree, with a temporal user-interaction graph attached.
notes: Items mined from real Reddit threads but labels are crowdsourced (529 Scale AI workers) - real-work-mined chosen for the items themselves. social-moral secondary is borderline (reading sarcasm/partial agreement is social signal). 'Closing' at 64% is soft: only 33% unanimous labels, so the true ceiling is below 100% and no frontier LLM was ever scored.
placement_reason: Built to answer whether models can read (dis)agreement in messy socio-political online debate — the raw material for studying polarization and opinion spread — making it the corpus's disagreement/social-judgment dataset (E3 names disagreement datasets explicitly); the alternative A1 stance-classification reading is real but the birth framing is social-dynamics-first, not generic NLU.

title: LHAW: Controllable Underspecification for Long-Horizon Tasks
human_time: Variants of TheAgentCompany, SWE-Bench Pro, and MCP-Atlas tasks - multi-stage workflows of dozens-to-hundreds of actions; a competent human would need roughly 30 min to several hours per instance.
frontier_score: Opus-4.5 on MCP-Atlas: 100% base -> 47% underspecified -> 78% pass@3 with ask_user; GPT-5.2 SWE-Bench Pro recovers to 91%
one_line: 285 controllably underspecified variants of long-horizon agent tasks that test whether agents notice missing info and ask well.
notes: Cross-domain meta-benchmark: tasks span office/agentic work (85 TAC), coding (100 SWE-Bench Pro), and MCP tool-use (100) - agentic-web primary is a judgment call. Provenance is an LLM perturbation pipeline over prior benchmarks (synthetic over aggregated base). 'Procedural' defense reflects the regenerable pipeline; the shipped 285 set itself is static. Browser access in TAC subset unstated in text.
placement_reason: Built to answer whether long-horizon agents know when to stop and ask a human for clarification, and at what interruption cost — the human-in-the-loop clarification behavior (ask_user tool, Supervisor vs Busy Executive personas) is the measured construct, which is D3's literal description; the synthetic perturbation pipeline is machinery for evaluation, not a training-signal factory (no F framing).

title: SWE Atlas: Benchmarking Coding Agents Beyond Issue Resolution
human_time: Codebase Q&A / test-writing / refactoring in 18 real repos, requiring running the app and multi-file edits; a competent engineer would need roughly 1-4 hours per task (refactors anchor to real merged PRs up to thousands of lines).
frontier_score: GPT-5.4 (Codex) 43.49% Pass@1, Opus 4.7 (Claude Code) 41.89%; Pass3 drops to ~29% for both
one_line: 284 expert-written tasks grading coding agents on codebase Q&A, test writing, and refactoring quality, not just passing tests.
notes: Pass requires functional gate AND all must-have rubrics; rubric-judge chosen as primary since the rubric is the distinguishing gate and the Q&A split (124 tasks) is rubric-only. Ships a memorization/leakage audit (Jaccard screen, tool-call audit) but no held-out or refreshed set, so defense = none-public.
placement_reason: Built to answer whether coding agents can do the software-engineering work beyond issue resolution — codebase Q&A, test writing, refactoring across 18 real repos — i.e. a capability probe on repo-level code understanding and modification; the rubric-vs-functional-gap finding gives it a B4 flavor, but the role at birth is measuring an unmeasured skill domain, not auditing an existing instrument.

title: SQuAD: 100,000+ Questions for Machine Comprehension of Text
human_time: Read one Wikipedia paragraph and highlight an answer span - tens of seconds per question for a human.
frontier_score: Human 86.8% F1 / 77.0% EM; neural models hit 70.3% F1 within four months and the human gap was 'erased entirely by 2018'
one_line: 107,785 crowdsourced questions over Wikipedia where every answer is a passage span, gradable by exact-match and token F1.
notes: Headline metric is token F1 (proxy-metric per rubric) but EM is a co-headline and the span design is 'exact-match gradable' - borderline primary call. Private-holdout is from own knowledge (official hidden test set via Codalab leaderboard), not stated in the text; the explainer now calls it fully contaminated/saturated.
placement_reason: The canonical span-extraction reading-comprehension probe — built to answer 'can neural models read?' by threading large + human-written + auto-gradable via the answer-is-a-passage-span constraint; SQuAD is a named A1 exemplar.

title: AndroidWorld: A Dynamic Benchmarking Environment for Autonomous Agents
human_time: Humans succeed 80% and agents average 3.9 min per task at ~3x human speed, so roughly 1-2 minutes per task for a human; most tasks are a handful of UI steps, tail 30+.
frontier_score: M3A (GPT-4 Turbo, accessibility-tree input) 30.6% vs 80.0% human; 3-seed mean 29.0%
one_line: Android emulator with 116 tasks across 20 real apps, graded by device system state and re-parameterized per random seed.
notes: Also ships 92 ported MobileMiniWoB++ web tasks on the same interface (not counted in items_count). Procedural defense = seeded task parameterization generating fresh goals/success criteria per run; templates and apps are still public. Mobile GUI control mapped to computer-os; domain-api for the send_text_message-style function-calling APIs. 30.6% is a 2024 single-seed number on zero-shot scaffolds.
placement_reason: Built to answer 'can agents operate a real phone?' — a fully functional Android emulator with execution-based reward on 116 tasks across 20 real apps, explicitly the mobile counterpart to OSWorld; the environment/agentic task is the contribution, and though parameterization enables RL training, the paper frames itself as a benchmarking environment, not a reward factory.

title: Do ImageNet Classifiers Generalize to ImageNet?
human_time: Single-image classification into one of the ImageNet/CIFAR-10 classes - a few seconds per image for a human.
frontier_score: Best 2018 model (PNASNet) 82.9% orig -> 72.2% top-1 on ImageNetV2 MatchedFrequency (an 10.7pp drop)
one_line: ImageNetV2 and CIFAR-10.1: from-scratch replications of the classic test sets that reveal an 11-14pp accuracy drop.
notes: Methodology paper; classified artifact is the replicated test sets (10,000 ImageNetV2 + 2,000 CIFAR-10.1 = 12,000). Items scraped from Flickr/Tiny Images then MTurk-filtered - crowdsourced chosen since worker selection frequency literally defines the item set. Fresh-at-release but public and static thereafter, hence none-public. Saturation judged from the explainer's 72.2% top-1; the paper is a measurement study, not a leaderboard.
placement_reason: The archetype rebuilt-twin audit: replicates the ImageNet and CIFAR-10 test-set creation process from scratch to measure adaptive overfitting from a decade of test-set reuse — the headline finding is about the measurement apparatus (benchmark validity, 'accuracy on the line'), not a new skill; ImageNet-v2-style replication is B1's named exemplar.

title: GPQA: A Graduate-Level Google-Proof Q&A Benchmark
human_time: One graduate-level MC science question; non-experts spent median 30 min with web, in-domain experts far less — a single hard multiple-choice item.
frontier_score: GPT-4 + search: 39.4% (Extended) at 2023 release; experts 65%, non-experts 34%, chance 25%
one_line: 448 graduate-level bio/physics/chemistry multiple-choice questions written by PhDs to be objective for experts yet Google-proof for non-experts.
notes: Canonical use is closed-book (none); paper also ran a GPT-4 + search-retrieval condition, not reflected in tool_access. Saturation marked 'closing' per the explainer's 'closing in on saturation' language for Diamond; external knowledge suggests Diamond is now near-saturated for frontier reasoning models, but the text only quotes 2023 numbers. Main-set count used (448); Extended is 546, Diamond 198.
placement_reason: Built to manufacture a 'Google-proof' expertise gap at the frontier of human knowledge: PhD-authored, expert-validated questions that skilled non-experts fail even with unrestricted web access, framed as a scalable-oversight testbed where the wall (durable expert-vs-non-expert gap) is the explicit design goal. The named C1 exemplar.

title: The MASK Benchmark: Disentangling Honesty From Accuracy in AI Systems
human_time: Reading a hand-crafted pressure scenario and answering a short factual (binary/numerical) proposition — roughly 1-5 min per item.
frontier_score: Most honest Claude 3.7 Sonnet P(Lie)=26.6%; worst Grok 2 63.0%; no model explicitly honest in >46% of cases
one_line: 1,500 pressure-scenario examples measuring whether an LLM knowingly lies (states what it doesn't believe), scored separately from factual accuracy.
notes: Grading: an LLM judge (86.4% human agreement) maps statement S and elicited belief B to proposition resolutions, then a lie is scored programmatically as S≠B. task_shape marked single-turn (belief elicitation is separate queries, not a dialogue), though 'Doubling Down'/'Continuations' archetypes are conversational. items_count is 1000 public + 500 held-out; paper reports on the combined 1500. Accuracy axis is genuinely measured, hence knowledge secondary.
placement_reason: Measures honesty-under-pressure as a propensity — whether a model knowingly states what it does not believe — cleanly split from accuracy; the object is deceptive-behavior risk, not a skill. Built explicitly against TruthfulQA-style 'honesty' evals that measure accuracy in disguise ('safetywashing'). Named E2 exemplar.

title: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
human_time: Labeling one image (classify among 1000 classes) takes seconds; the trained human annotator did 1500 images after a short training phase.
frontier_score: GoogLeNet 6.7% top-5 error (2014); trained human A1 5.1% on the same sample
one_line: 1000-class ImageNet image-classification challenge (plus single-object localization and 200-class detection), scored by top-5 error on a withheld test set.
notes: Classification top-5 error is label exact-match; localization/detection use box-IoU/mAP (programmatic), hence grading_all includes programmatic. Provenance crowdsourced (web images labeled via Amazon Mechanical Turk with QC voting). items_count ≈ 100,000 test images; training set is ~1.2M. Treated as saturated: 2014 machine error already near the single-human baseline and long since surpassed.
placement_reason: Role at birth: give the ImageNet database the protocol it lacked — a fixed 1000-class task, withheld test set, evaluation server, and one metric — so 'is visual recognition getting better?' became answerable. The canonical perception capability probe of the deep-learning era.

title: PropensityBench: Evaluating Latent Safety Risks in Large Language Models via an Agentic Approach
human_time: The underlying task τ is a short multi-step tool operation; a scenario runs up to 156 agent interactions across 12 pressure levels, but the equivalent human work per instance is minutes (synthetic agentic task).
frontier_score: Avg propensity 46.9% at max pressure; safest OpenAI o3 10.5%, worst Gemini 2.5 Pro 79.0% (near-zero at zero pressure)
one_line: 5,874 agentic scenarios that rig the safe tool to always fail and escalate operational pressure, measuring an LLM's propensity to grab a forbidden dangerous-capability tool.
notes: Headline propensity is a mechanical/programmatic check (did the agent ever call the misaligned tool?); LLM judges are used in scenario generation and the justification taxonomy (hence llm-judge in grading_all). Contamination: scenarios are freshly synthesized and the metric is behavioral (low exposure), but no explicit held-out/canary/live-refresh mechanism is described and templated pressure prompts are Goodhart-able, so marked none-public — low confidence on this dimension. Saturation 'open' interpreted as the safety target (near-zero propensity) being far from met; could be argued not-applicable since it's a lower-is-better propensity metric. Complexity is a judgment call on synthetic agentic tasks.
placement_reason: Shifts frontier-safety evaluation from can-do to would-do: hands an agent a forbidden proxy tool and escalating operational pressure to measure its propensity to knowingly cross a line across bio/chem/cyber/self-proliferation risk domains. The object is latent risk, not skill. Named E2 exemplar.

title: SciPredict: Can LLMs Predict the Outcomes of Scientific Experiments in Natural Sciences?
human_time: Reading one fully-specified experimental setup and predicting its outcome — an expert reasoning task of roughly minutes to tens of minutes per item.
frontier_score: Gemini 3 Pro best at ~25.3%; range 14-26% across 15 models, vs ~20% human-expert baseline
one_line: 405 post-training-cutoff physics/chemistry/biology experiment-outcome prediction tasks testing predictive accuracy and, centrally, calibration.
notes: Grading is a composite: MCQ (~40%, exact-match), numerical (~28%, expert tolerance-band check — treated as numeric exact-match), free-form (~32%, LLM rubric-judge). Primary set to exact-match as the plurality/headline format, but rubric-judge is central — this dimension is a judgment call. Contamination is live-refresh by design (post-March-2025 papers, web search disabled; rubric names SciPredict as the example). Provenance marked expert-authored (recruited PhD experts write tasks/rubrics/tolerance bands over 7,380 expert-hours), though items are grounded in real recent papers (real-work-mined flavor). Domain science with strong forecasting/calibration character (secondary).
placement_reason: Post-cutoff sourcing is the stated core design, not an add-on: every task comes from papers posted after every evaluated model's training cutoff with web search disabled, so 'prediction' can never collapse into recall — unsaturable-by-construction outcome prediction. The taxonomy's own C3 style exemplar ('SciPredict-style').

title: ResearchRubrics: A Benchmark of Prompts and Rubrics For Evaluating Deep Research Agents
human_time: Producing one well-researched 5k-50k-token report on an open-ended prompt via multi-source web research — several hours of expert work.
frontier_score: Gemini DR 0.677 (ternary) / 0.615 (binary); OpenAI DR 0.664 / 0.597; no system tops 70%
one_line: 101 open-ended Deep-Research-agent prompts paired with 2,593 expert-written weighted rubric criteria, LLM-judged for compliance.
notes: domain_primary is a judgment call: the evaluated systems are web-research (Deep Research) agents (agentic-web), but the graded skill is implicit reasoning + cross-document synthesis of a written report (reasoning/knowledge secondary). task_shape marked interactive-env because DR agents run a live multi-step web-browse/retrieve loop, though the human-facing prompt is single-turn and the harness only grades the final report. Grading is rubric-judge (LLM over per-criterion weighted rubrics); human annotators validate the judge but don't score systems. No private held-out set, live-web retrieval is non-reproducible, prompts adapted from public forums — hence none-public. Best systems ~62-68% → closing.
placement_reason: Built to answer 'how good are Deep Research agents at open-ended, multi-source research?' — the headline is agent skill (no system clears 70%; failures concentrate in implicit reasoning and cross-document synthesis), with the 2,593 hand-written rubrics as the instrument. The binary-vs-ternary grading findings pull toward G3, but they are secondary lessons, so per the tie-break the skill headline wins.

title: Language Models are Multilingual Chain-of-Thought Reasoners
human_time: 1-5 min per item: a GSM8K-style grade-school word problem needing 2-8 reasoning steps (paper's own step counts).
frontier_score: PaLM-540B 55.0% avg (TRANSLATE-EN best setting; 2022 numbers)
one_line: 250 GSM8K math problems hand-translated into 10 languages to test multilingual chain-of-thought reasoning.
notes: Provenance borderline: items are GSM8K (crowdsourced) problems re-rendered by hired professional translators; could also read as aggregated. items_count = 250 x 11 languages incl. English. Saturation from own knowledge (frontier models score ~90%+ by 2024); explainer only quotes 2022 numbers.
placement_reason: Born to answer 'can step-by-step reasoning that works in English survive in Swahili/Bengali/Telugu?' — the first multilingual multi-step arithmetic benchmark, literally 250 GSM8K problems hand-translated into ten languages with checkable numeric answers. Headline is model performance on a frontier reasoning skill, so A3 (GSM8K family), not A2: the object is math reasoning, not exam knowledge.

title: Large Language Models Encode Clinical Knowledge
human_time: ~1-2 min per USMLE-style vignette; several minutes for a long-form consumer health answer.
frontier_score: Flan-PaLM 540B 67.6% on MedQA (USMLE), +17 over prior SOTA
one_line: MultiMedQA: seven medical QA sets plus clinician-rated axes (consensus, harm) pushing medical eval past multiple choice.
notes: Classified artifact = MultiMedQA benchmark + clinician human-eval framework (Med-PaLM the model is the vehicle). Headline metric is MC accuracy; the 12-axis human panel is the lasting methodological piece. items_count (~11k across the seven sets) and saturation (MedQA >90% for current frontier) from own knowledge, not the explainer.
placement_reason: The gap is explicitly clinical-deployment readiness: exam accuracy 'says nothing about whether a free-text answer is factual, complete, or dangerous' and 'medicine is the wrong domain to evaluate sloppily.' MultiMedQA plus the 12-axis clinician harm/consensus/bias framework asks whether answers are safe enough for the clinic — occupational medical competence, the HealthBench-precursor role the D2 rubric names. Not A2: the multiple-choice exams are the thing it argues past; not G3: the human-eval axes serve the medical question, the protocol isn't the headline.

title: MultiChallenge: A Realistic Multi-Turn Conversation Evaluation Benchmark Challenging to Frontier LLMs
human_time: A few minutes: read a ~1,232-word, ~5-turn conversation and answer the final turn (paper's own averages).
frontier_score: Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Jun 2024) 41.42% human-eval average
one_line: 273 adversarially-mined multi-turn conversations testing instruction retention, memory, editing, and self-coherence.
notes: Multi-turn by content but the model answers only the final turn of a frozen history (no live user simulation) - borderline single-turn. Provenance is MMSE multi-agent synthesis, then heavy human editing (~25% rewritten). A held-private hard split exists but isn't scored, so contamination defense counted as none-public (the 273 are on GitHub).
placement_reason: The gap opens with saturation: 'MT-Bench and MT-Eval — once the standard — are saturated... A benchmark that everyone passes measures nothing,' and the design gate keeps only examples that break >=3 of 6 frontier models. Same construct (multi-turn conversation ability) rebuilt 'deliberately un-saturated' for discriminative power — the exact Arena-Hard-vs-MT-Bench pattern B3 names. The skill isn't new; the restored headroom is the point.

title: TutorBench: A Benchmark To Assess Tutoring Capabilities Of Large Language Models
human_time: ~5-15 min: read the tutoring exchange plus (often) handwritten student work and craft a pedagogically sound response.
frontier_score: Gemini 2.5 Pro 55.65% overall; GPT-5 55.33%
one_line: 1,490 STEM tutoring conversations (half with handwritten-work images) rubric-graded on explanation, feedback, hints.
notes: Tutoring/pedagogy -> social-moral per rubric (the tested skill is interpersonal teaching, not STEM solving - the difficulty filter enforces that). Frozen-conversation final-response grading makes the multi-turn shape borderline single-turn. The 3-of-5-models difficulty filter is an anti-saturation device, not a contamination defense; 30 samples public, full release planned, no private holdout.
placement_reason: The gap is a shipping question: 'LLMs are already de-facto tutors for millions of students... If we are going to deploy these systems as teachers, we need to measure how good they actually are at teaching.' The construct is tutoring-as-service with a student in the loop — adapt to confusion, give feedback, hint without revealing — exactly the tutoring/teacher-assist benchmark D3 names. Not A7: the framing is deployment of a teaching service, not conversational skill per se; not A3: solving the problem is explicitly what it is NOT measuring.

title: Beyond the Imitation Game: Quantifying and Extrapolating the Capabilities of Language Models
human_time: Typically <1 min: one MC or short-answer item; some tasks (logic puzzles, multi-step arithmetic) run a few minutes.
frontier_score: best 2022 models < 20 on 0-100 human-normalized scale (experts ~100); PaLM ~42 on BIG-bench Lite
one_line: 204 community-contributed tasks designed to outpace models and chart which capabilities emerge with scale.
notes: items_count = 204 tasks (per-task example counts vary widely; total examples not stated). Provenance: open GitHub community contributions, though mostly by researchers. A minority of programmatic Python tasks are multi-turn. Saturation from own knowledge - community treats BIG-bench/BBH as solved (BBEH built to replace it); the <20 headline is a 2022 number.
placement_reason: The G1 archetype by name: a 204-task collaborative compendium from 450 authors across 132 institutions, whose contribution is a WAY of evaluating — evaluation as capability forecasting (linearity vs breakthroughness, canary contamination guard, human-normalized aggregation) — not any single task skill. The gap section is about benchmarks themselves (SuperGLUE's two-year saturation treadmill, smooth aggregates hiding emergence), and the point 'is not to crown a winner but to chart what models can't yet do.'

title: PaperBench: Evaluating AI's Ability to Replicate AI Research
human_time: Days-plus: ML PhDs reached only 41.4% after 48 h and were still climbing; a full replication is plausibly 1-2 weeks.
frontier_score: o1 IterativeAgent 24.4% (26.0% at 36 h); Claude 3.5 Sonnet BasicAgent 21.0%
one_line: Agents replicate 20 ICML 2024 papers from scratch, graded on 8,316 author-co-written rubric criteria by an LLM judge.
notes: Provenance: task items are real published ICML papers (real-work-mined), with rubrics expert/author-co-written. Explainer mentions an internal held-out paper set -> private-holdout, though the scored 20 are public with a self-acknowledged contamination clock. reproduce.sh executes in a fresh env but every leaf is judge-scored, so grading stays rubric-judge.
placement_reason: Asks 'can an AI agent read a fresh ML paper and autonomously reproduce it — build the codebase, run the experiments, match the numbers?' — open-ended ML research work end-to-end, the MLE-bench-style skill A8 names. The gap is that CORE-Bench/MLE-bench/RE-Bench don't capture the full from-scratch loop. Preparedness/RSP autonomy tracking is a motivation, but the headline (best agent 21.0% vs PhDs 41.4%) is skill performance, not a risk finding, so A8 over E2.

title: Reasoning Gym: infinite, verifiable reasoning environments for RLVR
human_time: A typical procedurally generated puzzle/math item (algebra, ListOps-style logic, small Rubik's cube) takes a human a few minutes; hard configs longer.
frontier_score: o3-mini 63.51% avg on hard RG configs (DeepSeek-R1 59.52%); hardest per-task configs top out under 8%
one_line: 100+ procedural generators of algorithmically verifiable reasoning puzzles with tunable difficulty, for RLVR and eval.
notes: Doubles as an RLVR training environment; item count is unbounded by design (100+ generators, infinite instances). Verifiers are algorithmic checkers over final answers, many reducing to exact match.
placement_reason: The gap section is explicit: RLVR's bottleneck is the supply of checkable problems, and RG is 'a library of procedural generators rather than a frozen dataset' built to emit unlimited verifiable training data at tunable difficulty. Training on it is the headline use (RL-training Qwen2.5-3B with GRPO on generator output); the eval leaderboard is secondary. Generator-as-contribution with explicit training intent is the F1 definition verbatim.

title: Long Range Arena: A Benchmark for Efficient Transformers
human_time: One instance is a classification over a 1K-16K-token input (byte-level review, nested ListOps expression, pixel image); a human needs minutes to read/resolve, though Pathfinder rendered visually is seconds.
frontier_score: BigBird 55.01 five-task avg vs vanilla Transformer 54.39; every 2020 model fails Path-X (16K) at chance
one_line: Six 1K-16K-token from-scratch tasks ranking efficient-Transformer architectures on long-range sequence modeling.
notes: Evaluates architectures trained from scratch, not LLMs. Saturation call uses outside knowledge: state-space models (S4 lineage, Mega) later solved Path-X and pushed averages to ~86-90, and the community treats LRA as retired; the explainer's own 2020 numbers read as open. Contamination is moot since the protocol forbids pretraining.
placement_reason: Built to answer 'which architecture can actually model long context?' — six encoding tasks from 1K to 16K tokens, trained from scratch so the score isolates long-range inductive bias. A long-context stress test / architecture capability probe, the A1 exemplar named in the taxonomy. Its in-corpus cites (NQ, MultiNLI, HotpotQA) are incidental; its real ancestry is the GLUE-style averaged multi-task suite method transplanted to the efficient-transformer subfield.

title: BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models
human_time: One instance is retrieving relevant documents for a single query from a large corpus; a human with search tooling would need minutes per query.
frontier_score: BM25+CE re-ranker beats BM25 on 16/18 datasets, +11% avg nDCG@10 (2021 snapshot); 0.757 on TREC-COVID
one_line: 18-dataset, 9-task zero-shot retrieval benchmark scoring retrievers out-of-domain by nDCG@10.
notes: Evaluates retrieval systems, not generative LLMs; tool_access = the retrieval corpus is the task substrate. Grading is automatic nDCG against human-pooled relevance labels (labels are provenance, not eval-time human grading). Saturation is ambiguous: nDCG ceiling is nominal (annotation holes), modern embedders reach ~0.55 avg, and the benchmark became a training target - 'open' is a loose call.
placement_reason: 'BEIR-style retrieval' is A1's named exemplar. Role at birth: can neural retrievers generalize zero-shot to unseen corpora? — a skill headline (BM25 vs dense out-of-domain), not an audit of a grader or a durability wall. It extends in-corpus component datasets (NQ, HotpotQA) into a unified 18-dataset zero-shot retrieval suite. The Hole@10 annotation-bias study is a B-flavored aside, but the paper's contribution and headline are the heterogeneous capability benchmark itself.

title: AppWorld: A Controllable World of Apps and People for Benchmarking Interactive Coding Agents
human_time: Solutions average 50 lines (max 134) of multi-app API code with control flow; a competent dev with the docs needs roughly 20-60 min per task - borderline L2/L3.
frontier_score: ReAct + GPT-4o: 48.8% TGC normal / 30.2% challenge (32.1/13.0 SGC), mid-2024 snapshot
one_line: 750 interactive coding tasks over 9 simulated apps and 457 APIs, graded on database end-state incl. collateral damage.
notes: 250 author-written scenarios x 3 programmatically instantiated variants; generators exist but the shipped set plus gold solutions is public on GitHub, so contamination defense = none-public (Test-C unseen-app split is a generalization guard, not a holdout). Complexity is borderline L2/L3. Saturation judged from the 2024 numbers; live leaderboard likely higher now.
placement_reason: The contribution is the ENVIRONMENT: a simulated world of 9 apps / 457 APIs / ~100 people with mutable database state, where agents operate the world through interactive code and are graded on the resulting state (collateral-damage clause). Built explicitly against trace-graded tool benchmarks (Gorilla, ToolLLM) and porting the WebArena/OSWorld interactive-environment style from web/GUI surfaces to a personal-app ecosystem — A5's app-environment case, not A4 plain code-writing or A6 tool-selection.

title: VeRO: An Evaluation Harness for Agents to Optimize Agents
human_time: One instance = iteratively editing an agent codebase across a budget of 8 gated evaluations, reading traces between edits; a human engineer would spend hours to a day per optimization run.
frontier_score: VeRO-Agent Default (Sonnet 4.5) lifts avg target score 0.50 -> 0.61 (+8 pts); GEPA and prompt-only variants ~0.54
one_line: Budget-gated, permission-controlled harness + 5-task benchmark measuring how well coding agents optimize another agent's code.
notes: Meta-benchmark: the optimizer is graded by the target agent's lift on 5 aggregated public benchmarks (MATH, TAU-Bench Retail, GAIA, SimpleQA, GPQA), whose own grading is exact-match/programmatic. 'Private-holdout' = harness access-controls the test split, though the underlying data is public and only SimpleQA is contamination-hardened. Saturation call is loose - no natural ceiling for 'optimization lift'.
placement_reason: The question at birth: how good are coding agents at the open-ended engineering task of optimizing another agent's program (edit, evaluate, version under budget)? That is ML/agent-engineering knowledge work, MLE-bench-adjacent — A8. The harness (versioning, budget gating, permission control) is infrastructure to measure that skill fairly, mirroring how SWE-bench's Docker harness serves its skill probe (the paper says so explicitly). Borderline with G (the title says 'evaluation harness' and the protocol is the deliverable), but the headline claims are about optimizer-agent performance on the task, so A wins the tie-break.

title: The AI Productivity Index (APEX)
human_time: Paper states mean 2.7 hours of real professional work per case (range 0.5-20h), per expert estimate.
frontier_score: GPT-5 (thinking=high) 67.0% of rubric criteria met; Gemini 3 Pro 64.3%; law easiest (77.9%), banking hardest (63.0%)
one_line: Expert-authored deliverables from banking, consulting, law and medicine, graded on Pass/Fail rubrics by an LLM judge.
notes: 137 vetted practitioners author source-grounded cases (~14.8 binary criteria each); single Gemini 2.5 Flash judge grades all criteria including Google's own models. n=400 scored set private, n=100 devset public; forced single-turn with attached source documents (up to 100K tokens) but no tools.
placement_reason: Named 'The AI Productivity Index' and framed against GDPval: does frontier work deliver real-world value on realistic deliverables from four high-paid jobs (banking, consulting, law, medicine), with tasks valued at ~2.7 expert-hours each. Explicit economic/productivity framing puts it in D1 rather than D2 despite the occupational content; its corpus neighborhood (cites GDPval + HealthBench, cited by RLI/UpBench/PRBench) is the economic-rehearsal cluster. The rubric-as-unit-tests grading imports HealthBench's physician-rubric method across occupations.

title: GLUE: A Multi-Task Benchmark and Analysis Platform for Natural Language Understanding
human_time: Seconds to ~1 min per item — single-sentence or sentence-pair classification judgments (acceptability, sentiment, paraphrase, NLI).
frontier_score: BERT-class models pushed past the human baseline (~87) within a year; best 2018 baseline (BiLSTM+Attn+ELMo multi-task) 70.0
one_line: Nine recycled English NLU classification tasks macro-averaged into one leaderboard score, with private test labels on four tasks.
notes: Grading folded to exact-match: STS-B is scored by Pearson/Spearman correlation and CoLA by Matthews correlation, which are aggregate classification/regression statistics rather than overlap proxies. Test-set item count not stated in the text. The ~550-pair expert-authored diagnostic is a secondary artifact within an otherwise aggregated benchmark.
placement_reason: Born to answer 'can one model do general language understanding across tasks?' — nine recycled NLU tasks (entailment, paraphrase, acceptability) collapsed into one model-agnostic leaderboard; the gap it fills is siloed per-dataset NLU evaluation, a classic parse/entail capability probe (the taxonomy's own A1 exemplar).

title: SuperGLUE: A Stickier Benchmark for General-Purpose Language Understanding Systems
human_time: Under a minute per item — short-passage QA/NLI/coreference/WSD designed to be solvable by college-educated non-experts (MTurk human baseline protocol).
frontier_score: T5-11B scored 88.9 within a year vs the 89.8 human estimate; models soon passed the human baseline outright (BERT++ launch baseline 71.5)
one_line: GLUE's harder sequel: eight difficulty-screened NLU tasks (QA, coreference, WSD) with crowd human baselines and a held-out test server.
notes: reasoning secondary for COPA causal reasoning and WSC/ReCoRD commonsense. Total test-item count not stated (per-task sizes range 146 to ~10k). Aggregated: all eight tasks recycle pre-existing public datasets chosen via open call.
placement_reason: Built explicitly because BERT/XLNet pushed GLUE past its human baseline within a year — same construct (multi-task NLU macro-average), rebuilt with difficulty-screened tasks to restore headroom; the taxonomy's canonical B3 case (SuperGLUE vs GLUE).

title: MAUD: An Expert-Annotated Legal NLP Dataset for Merger Agreement Understanding
human_time: ~2-10 min per item for a trained M&A attorney to read a deal-point clause (half exceed 512 tokens; MAE clauses much longer) and answer the ABA-standardized multiple-choice question; estimated from task descriptions and the 10,000-hour/$500-per-hour annotation effort.
frontier_score: BigBird-base 57.8% overall AUPR (best single-task encoder baseline) vs 16.8% random; hardest categories near 48-51%
one_line: Expert-annotated legal reading comprehension: 144 ABA-standardized multiple-choice questions over deal points in 152 real merger agreements.
notes: Provenance is borderline: clause texts are mined from real EDGAR merger agreements and questions derive from the ABA's existing deal-point study, but the defining effort is 10,000+ hours of recruited expert annotation, so expert-authored over real-work-mined. items_count is the derived test split (~20% of the 35,551 main+abridged examples), not stated verbatim. Headline metric is minority-class AUPR over classifier scores, folded to exact-match (MC classification). Saturation judged from 2023 encoder baselines only — no frontier LLM was evaluated.
placement_reason: Expert-annotated M&A contract-review benchmark ($5M of lawyer time, ABA deal-point questions) probing the interpretive judgment M&A attorneys are paid for — occupational law/contracts content beats its 'can models read legal prose' skill framing per the tie-break ladder.

title: MMLU-Pro: A More Robust and Challenging Multi-Task Language Understanding Benchmark
human_time: ~1-10 min per item — ten-option exam questions where the added STEM material (stemez/TheoremQA/SciBench) demands multi-step CoT derivations rather than recall.
frontier_score: GPT-4o 72.6% overall (best at launch, vs ~88.7% on MMLU); reasoning models (o1, R1) already climbing it by 2025
one_line: The reasoning-era rebuild of MMLU: 12,032 ten-option questions across 14 domains, filtered for difficulty and expert-reviewed twice.
notes: Aggregated: 56.6% reworked MMLU plus STEM-website/TheoremQA/SciBench items, with GPT-4-Turbo-authored distractors under expert review (synthetic-generated is only the distractor pool, not the stems). Fully public and 56.6% derived from the most-leaked benchmark ever, so none-public despite the expert cleanup.
placement_reason: MMLU had flatlined at 86-87% for every frontier model; MMLU-Pro rebuilds the same construct (broad multi-task MC knowledge exam) with 10 options, easy-item filtering and harder STEM material to restore discriminative headroom — the taxonomy's named B3 case (MMLU-Pro vs MMLU).

title: EnigmaEval: A Benchmark of Long Multimodal Reasoning Challenges
human_time: Typical Normal puzzle ~15-60 min (88% are PuzzledPint, whose guideline is an inexperienced team finishing the whole event under 2 hours); Hard puzzles take experienced teams hours to days, i.e. L3+.
frontier_score: o1 solves 7.0% of Normal and 0.0% of Hard; all nine models score 0% on Hard, next-best (Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking) 1.4%
one_line: 1,184 instruction-free multimodal puzzle-hunt problems requiring lateral, cross-domain inference, graded by exact string match.
notes: Complexity is bimodal: Normal (949 items, dominated by beginner PuzzledPint) is L2-minutes, Hard (235) is L3-hours-plus; classified by the typical instance. Contamination defense combines a private unreleased set with continuous updates — live-refresh is the strongest mechanism; private-holdout also applies. exam-derived: items come from existing real puzzle-hunt competitions.
placement_reason: Framed as joining HLE in 'a new class of deliberately unsaturated benchmark' — puzzle-hunt problems where the best model scores 7%/0%, kept private and continuously refreshed; durable headroom at the frontier is the explicit design goal (a named C1 exemplar).

title: MultiFileTest: A Multi-File-Level LLM Unit Test Generation Benchmark and Impact of Error Fixing Mechanisms
human_time: ~1-4 hours for a competent developer to write a runnable, high-coverage unit-test suite for a 2-15-file, <1,600-LOC project.
frontier_score: Gemini-3.0-Pro 77% vanilla correctness on Python (90 after manual fixing); GPT-o1 87% on JavaScript; Java hardest with several 7B models at 0
one_line: Unit-test generation over 60 whole multi-file Python/Java/JS projects, scored by real execution, with a vanilla vs error-fixed comparison.
notes: Task shape: headline (vanilla) scenario is one-shot generation with execution only for grading, so single-turn; the self-fixing scenario adds one execution-feedback round, edging toward interactive-env but not the primary protocol. Human manual fixing is an intervention scenario, not a grading mechanism. Saturation 'closing' judged on best vanilla correctness (62-87% by language).
placement_reason: Asks 'can models write runnable unit tests for whole multi-file projects?' — the gap is that HumanEval/ClassEval/TestGenEval grade test-gen on single functions/files; headline is model skill at cross-file code understanding and test writing (the error-fixing study is a diagnostic within the skill probe, not the object).

title: Measuring short-form factuality in large language models
human_time: Seconds to under a minute per item: one short single-answer fact question (lookup-style long-tail trivia).
frontier_score: o1-preview 42.7% correct (F-score 44.8); GPT-4o 38.2%, all frontier models under 50%
one_line: 4,326 short single-answer fact questions, adversarially filtered to stump GPT-4-class models, scoring abstention as a first-class outcome.
notes: Grading is a prompted ChatGPT equivalence check against a short gold string — coded exact-match primary with llm-judge in grading_all. Hired OpenAI AI trainers coded as expert-authored rather than crowdsourced.
placement_reason: The gap section is explicit: TriviaQA and Natural Questions are 'saturated — too easy for frontier models to be discriminative', and SimpleQA rebuilds the same short-form factual-QA construct adversarially (every question stumps at least one GPT-4-class model) to restore headroom. The calibration/abstention framing adds a B4 flavor, but the stated reason for building is de-saturation of the short-form factuality construct.

title: BrowseComp: A Simple Yet Challenging Benchmark for Browsing Agents
human_time: Human trainers solved only 29.2% within a 2-hour cap and gave up on 70.8%; solved items often took 2-3 hours — hours per question.
frontier_score: Deep Research 51.5% (best-of-64 confidence voting ~78%); GPT-4o 0.6%, o1 9.9%
one_line: 1,266 inverted-fact questions — trivial to verify, brutal to find — probing persistent web-browsing agents.
notes: Coded interactive-env/browser for the intended browsing-agent setting, though non-browsing baselines were also run (they score ~0-10%). Short-string LLM equivalence grading (HLE prompt) coded exact-match primary + llm-judge. 51.5% single-sample keeps it open; best-of-N ~78% is a nonstandard protocol.
placement_reason: The defining C2 exemplar: inverted questions engineered so the answer is a trivially verifiable short string but agonizingly hard to locate — vetted to stump GPT-4o/o1/early Deep Research, resist five Google searches, and defeat the human trainers themselves (70.8% gave up after two hours). Durable difficulty via the find/verify asymmetry is the explicit design goal, built because HotpotQA/TriviaQA-style 'findable' QA had saturated.

title: A Broad-Coverage Challenge Corpus for Sentence Understanding through Inference
human_time: Seconds per premise-hypothesis pair — crowdworkers label 3-way entailment in well under a minute.
frontier_score: BERT hit 86.7% within a year; later models low-90s — SuperGLUE retired it as near-solved (human agreement ~88.7%)
one_line: 433k premise-hypothesis NLI pairs across ten genres with a matched-vs-mismatched split testing cross-genre generalization.
notes: Test labels withheld on Kaggle → private-holdout, though the premises come from public corpora. items_count is the 20k test set (plus 20k dev; 433k pairs total including train).
placement_reason: Born as the harder, broader successor to SNLI: the gap names 'no headroom — the best models were already within a few points of human accuracy' and single-genre narrowness as the two limits, and rebuilds the identical three-way NLI construct across ten genres to restore ~15 points of headroom (ESIM 86.7 on SNLI falls to 72.3). Exactly the SuperGLUE-vs-GLUE de-saturation pattern, one generation earlier.

title: FinBen: A Holistic Financial Benchmark for Large Language Models
human_time: Typical item is a short financial classification/extraction (seconds) up to numeric QA over filings (a few minutes); the trading-agent episodes run much longer but are a small subset.
frontier_score: GPT-4 leads (FinQA EM 0.63, ConvFinQA 0.76; trading Sharpe 1.51) but forecasting MCC ~0 and many tasks score 0.00
one_line: 36-dataset, 24-task financial suite spanning extraction to forecasting, plus the first agent-graded stock-trading eval.
notes: Heterogeneous multi-task suite: grading spans F1/EM/MCC (exact-match), ROUGE/BERTScore (proxy), and simulator-scored trading returns (programmatic); most tasks are pure zero-shot text but the RAG and FinMem trading-agent subsets add retrieval/agent tooling, reflected in tool_access. Test sets are 100-4,000 items each across 36 datasets; total not stated.
placement_reason: A professional-finance competence suite: 36 datasets over the seven things 'financial LLMs are asked to do' — extraction, sentiment, numeric QA, risk management, forecasting, and agent-graded stock trading scored in money metrics (Sharpe, drawdown). Occupational content is the whole design ('measuring the actual job', not the easy 20%); the holistic-taxonomy shape gives a G1 pull, but domain-occupational competence beats framing per the ladder, and trading-for-profit is deployment-realism, not aggregation.

title: OSWorld: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Open-Ended Tasks in Real Computer Environments
human_time: Median human completion 111.94 s per task; the hard tier is >180 s — a few minutes each.
frontier_score: best 2024 agent (GPT-4 + a11y tree) 12.24% vs human 72.36%; screenshot-only ~5%
one_line: VM-backed real-computer environment: 369 execution-graded OS/app/multi-app tasks with per-task end-state checks.
notes: Task ideas mined from public tutorials/forums but configs and 134 eval scripts authored by the team → expert-authored. No shipped defense (none-public), though execution grading on a fresh VM inherently resists answer memorization.
placement_reason: The family's namesake style: the contribution is the ENVIRONMENT — a VM-backed real OS with 369 execution-graded tasks over a raw keyboard/mouse action space, built because static demonstration sets (Mind2Web) can't grade alternative solutions and executable environments (WebShop/WebArena) were boxed into the web. Headline is skill: can agents operate a real computer yet (humans 72.36% vs best agent 12.24%, grounding is the wall).

title: HotpotQA: A Dataset for Diverse, Explainable Multi-hop Question Answering
human_time: Roughly 1-3 minutes: read ~10 short Wikipedia paragraphs (2 gold + 8 distractors), chain two facts, extract a span; borderline with L1 for easy items.
frontier_score: baseline 58.28 distractor answer F1 vs human 91.40; leaderboard hit high-70s/80s joint-F1 within ~2 years — effectively solved
one_line: 113k crowd-written Wikipedia questions requiring two-document multi-hop chains, graded jointly on answer and supporting-fact evidence.
notes: Primary domain is a judgment call: classic NLU-era extractive QA (language, per the SQuAD precedent) with multi-hop reasoning and world knowledge secondary. tool_access reflects the fullwiki retrieval setting; the distractor setting is pure provided-context text. Hidden-test leaderboard (private-holdout) is from own knowledge of the paper, not stated in the explainer. items_count is one 7,405-item test set (distractor and fullwiki each; 112,779 pairs total).
placement_reason: A classic NLU capability probe at birth: QA had a 'depth problem' — SQuAD answerable from one span — so HotpotQA asks whether models can chain facts across two documents (plus retrieval in fullwiki) and justify the answer with supporting sentences. Multi-hop reading comprehension / retrieval / span extraction is squarely the SQuAD-family parsing skill, extended; the open frontier skill is the headline (58.28 F1 vs human 91.40), not durability or auditing.

title: WebShop: Towards Scalable Real-World Web Interaction with Grounded Language Agents
human_time: Humans averaged ~815 s (~14 min) per live-Amazon episode; a typical sandbox shopping task is minutes of search/compare/choose.
frontier_score: IL+RL agent 62.4 task score / 28.7% success vs 59.6% human-expert success
one_line: Simulated Amazon (1.18M real listings) where agents search, pick options, and buy; auto-graded by attribute/option/price spec match.
notes: Instructions crowd-written on MTurk over scraped real products (provenance crowdsourced, not real-work-mined). items_count is the full 12,087-instruction set; the standard test split is 500. Explainer only quotes 2022 pre-LLM agents (~29%); later LLM agents improved but the benchmark stayed unsaturated.
placement_reason: Built to answer 'can grounded agents operate a realistic web environment?' — prior interactive benchmarks were toy vocabularies (MiniWoB) or human-graded (WebGPT), so WebShop supplies a 1.18M-real-product e-commerce environment with an automatically computable reward. The environment/agentic task is the contribution; the taxonomy's A5 archetype is literally 'WebShop-style'. RL training inside it exists but the paper's headline is the human-agent capability gap (29% vs 60%), not a reward factory.

title: UpBench: A Dynamically Evolving Real-World Labor-Market Agentic Benchmark Framework Built for Human-Centric AI
human_time: Real fixed-price single-milestone Upwork gigs (web dev, data science, writing, translation); estimated hours to ~a day each — the text gives payouts, not hours.
frontier_score: Claude Sonnet 4: 39.8% first-attempt strict completion (51.2% after one human-feedback pass)
one_line: 322 real paid Upwork jobs graded by expert freelancers against 5-20 labeled acceptance criteria, with human-in-the-loop re-attempts.
notes: Grading is per-criterion rubrics scored by HUMAN experts, so human (not rubric-judge, which the taxonomy reserves for LLM graders). Agents run a plan-act-reflect loop with only attachment-read/artifact-store tools (no web/retrieval) — borderline interactive-env. 'Dynamically evolving' refresh is the design claim; the released 322-job snapshot is static. Complexity L3 is an estimate; some jobs may be L4.
placement_reason: 322 real, paid Upwork contracts with actual client payouts, an explicit economic breakeven model (E(V)=sV−c, AI/HITL/human regimes), and a continuously refreshed live-marketplace stream — squarely 'real paid work with explicit economic framing/valuation (live freelance feeds)'. The human-in-the-loop rescue axis gives it a D3 collaboration flavor, but the substrate and valuation framing are economic rehearsal.

title: MMMU: A Massive Multi-Discipline Multimodal Understanding and Reasoning Benchmark for Expert AGI
human_time: One college-exam question with image(s) requiring domain knowledge plus deliberate reasoning; ~1-5 min for a subject expert.
frontier_score: GPT-4o 69.1 (validation) vs 88.6 human experts; GPT-4V 55.7 test at launch
one_line: 11.5K college-exam questions across 30 subjects and 30 image types (diagrams, scans, scores) testing expert multimodal reasoning.
notes: Contamination call: 10.5K test-split answers are withheld behind a leaderboard (private-holdout-ish), but questions are public and textbook-sourced — explainer stresses this defense is weak. 94% MC scored by regex extraction with random fill on parse failure.
placement_reason: Explicitly the multimodal MMLU: 11.5K college-exam questions across 30 subjects testing expert knowledge + deliberate reasoning under exam conditions, framed against 'Expert AGI' (90th-percentile skilled adult). A2 names MMMU as an example. Born because VQA-style suites were saturated (~90%) while text exam suites (MMLU, AGIEval) showed no images.

title: HellaSwag: Can a Machine Really Finish Your Sentence?
human_time: One four-way sentence-completion choice; humans answer near-instantly (95.6% accuracy).
frontier_score: GPT-4 95.3% (10-shot) vs 95.6% human
one_line: Adversarially-filtered commonsense sentence completion: GPT-generated wrong endings BERT can't catch, trivial for humans.
notes: Provenance is mixed: real ActivityNet/WikiHow contexts and gold endings, but the defining task items (distractors) are GPT-1-generated via Adversarial Filtering with crowd validation — classified synthetic-generated. Fully public, in modern pretraining corpora, treated as saturated/retired.
placement_reason: Born the moment BERT 'solved' SWAG overnight; the paper shows the predecessor was gamed via distributional artifacts (ending-only 74.8%, shuffled 60.4%) and rebuilds the same commonsense-completion construct with adversarial filtering against BERT-Large to restore a >50-point human-machine gap — the textbook SuperGLUE-vs-GLUE desaturation sequel. SWAG is not a corpus node, so lineage is empty.

title: INJECAGENT: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated Large Language Model Agents
human_time: One decision point: read a tool response with an embedded malicious instruction and choose the next action — seconds for a human.
frontier_score: fine-tuned GPT-4: 6.6% ASR (base); ReAct-prompted GPT-4 hijacked 23.6% (47.0% enhanced)
one_line: 1,054 indirect-prompt-injection cases testing whether tool-using agents execute malicious instructions hidden in tool outputs.
notes: task_shape borderline: nothing executes — the benchmark inspects the parsed next action, with at most a 2-step GPT-4-simulated tool loop for data-stealing; could be argued single-turn. Saturation inverted metric: 'solved' would be ~0% ASR; fine-tuned models reach 4-8% but prompted frontier agents remain highly vulnerable — called open. Cases GPT-4-synthesized over ToolEmu schemas (low leak by construction but no ongoing defense).
placement_reason: First benchmark to formalize and measure indirect prompt injection against tool-integrated agents (1,054 cases, ASR as the metric) — the object is the attack surface a tool-calling agent exposes, not a skill. E1 names InjecAgent explicitly. Built in response to the tool-use agent wave whose action-level safety no benchmark measured; AgentDojo is its corpus descendant.

title: A Careful Examination of Large Language Model Performance on Grade School Arithmetic
human_time: ~3-4 min per problem: annotators solved 4.36 problems per 15-minute timer in the paper's own solve-rate check.
frontier_score: GPT-4o 92.9% on GSM1k (5-shot, fixed harness)
one_line: 1,205 hand-written, difficulty-matched private twin of GSM8k measuring how much contamination inflates public math scores.
notes: Provenance borderline: written by paid Scale annotators under a three-layer QA gauntlet (not domain experts, not an open crowd) - counted as expert-authored. The artifact is a math benchmark, but its lasting contribution is the private difficulty-matched twin methodology for contamination measurement. Classified manually by the lead agent after the batch-19 classifier skipped it (it classified mt-bench, batch 20's paper, instead).
placement_reason: Built to answer 'is GSM8k measuring reasoning or memorization?' — a hand-written, difficulty-matched, deliberately-withheld twin of GSM8k whose headline finding (overfit families drop up to 8 points, frontier models flat) is about the measurement apparatus, not a new skill; the explainer names Recht et al.'s CIFAR-10/ImageNet rebuilds as the imported template. The archetypal contamination twin.

title: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena
human_time: One two-turn open-ended chat question (writing, roleplay, math, coding); a few minutes to answer well.
frontier_score: GPT-4 8.99/10 at release; explainer: modern scores 'cluster near the ceiling'
one_line: 80 hand-written two-turn chat questions graded 1-10 by a GPT-4 judge; shipped alongside the Chatbot Arena human-vote leaderboard.
notes: Eval-methodology paper shipping two artifacts; classified the MT-bench set (per slug). Chatbot Arena would differ: user-traffic provenance, human/Elo grading, not-applicable saturation, live-refresh by design — human grading and its user-traffic character are reflected only in grading_all.
placement_reason: The contribution is preference machinery, not a task skill: LLM-as-a-judge validated against human votes, plus MT-bench (80 pairwise-judged questions) and Chatbot Arena (crowdsourced Elo battles). Born because MMLU/HELM could not distinguish aligned chat models from their bases; the headline claim (GPT-4 judge agrees with humans 85%) is about the evaluating method itself.

title: WebGuard: Building a Generalizable Guardrail for Web Agents
human_time: Judging one proposed web action's SAFE/LOW/HIGH label from a screenshot or A11y tree takes well under a minute; annotation collection took longer but the per-instance task is a single classification.
frontier_score: GPT-4o best zero-shot at 57.5% average three-class accuracy; fine-tuned WebGuard-VL-7B reaches 80.4%
one_line: Action-level web-agent safety dataset: classify 4,939 real-website actions as SAFE/LOW/HIGH by reversibility and blast radius.
notes: Provenance is borderline: actions are harvested from 193 real live websites but the items/labels are created by trained, pilot-qualified annotators, so expert-authored over real-work-mined. Evaluated model gets page state as input only (no browsing), so tool_access none.
placement_reason: An action-level unsafe-action-guard dataset: 4,939 real-website actions graded SAFE/LOW/HIGH by reversibility and blast radius, built to train/measure a guardrail that blocks harmful web-agent actions before execution — E1's 'unsafe-action guards / refusal-of-harmful-web-actions' exactly. The object is risk of consequential actions, not task skill; guardrail fine-tuning serves the safety instrument.

title: AGIEval: A Human-Centric Benchmark for Evaluating Foundation Models
human_time: One exam item (SAT/LSAT/Gaokao MC or fill-in) takes a human test-taker roughly 1-3 minutes, per standard exam pacing.
frontier_score: GPT-4 zero-shot CoT all-task average 58.4 (few-shot CoT 61.3); 95.0% on SAT-Math
one_line: 8,062 bilingual multiple-choice/fill-in questions repurposed from 20 official high-stakes human exams (Gaokao, SAT, LSAT, bar, AMC/AIME).
notes: Saturation call uses own knowledge beyond the 2023 numbers quoted (modern frontier models score well above GPT-4's 61.3, but sub-90 on the hard subsets) -> closing. F1 on fill-in-blank counted as proxy-metric in grading_all.
placement_reason: Repurposes 20 official high-stakes human exams (Gaokao, SAT, LSAT, GRE/GMAT, Chinese bar) into 8,062 bilingual objective questions to ask 'how does the model stack up against a person under exam conditions?' — headline is model-vs-human exam performance. Built against MMLU's opaque provenance and English-only scope and against GLUE-era single-skill curation; a named A2 exemplar.

title: Instruction-Following Evaluation for Large Language Models
human_time: Each prompt is an ordinary writing task plus checkable constraints (e.g. 400+ words in JSON); a human would need several minutes to draft a compliant response.
frontier_score: GPT-4 (Nov 2023) 76.89% prompt-level strict accuracy
one_line: 541 prompts with deterministically verifiable constraints (word counts, JSON, keywords, casing) scoring instruction compliance judge-free.
notes: Saturation judged from the explainer's own framing ('now easy to saturate and partly contaminated', sits in post-training mixes) plus community knowledge of >90% frontier scores, not from the 2023 numbers quoted.
placement_reason: Built to answer 'can models actually follow instructions precisely?' via 541 prompts of deterministically-verifiable directives (word counts, JSON, casing) — the headline is model compliance on the instruction-following skill (GPT-4 fails ~1 prompt in 4). The judge-free verifier design is the method, but the object measured is the skill; the taxonomy's own A7 exemplar ('IFEval-style').

title: Agent-as-a-Judge: Evaluate Agents with Agents
human_time: One DevAI task is a small end-to-end AI build (load data, train a model, report, visualize) kept 'small enough to run cheaply' - a competent ML engineer would need roughly 1-4 hours.
frontier_score: GPT-Pilot / OpenHands satisfy ~29% of requirements with dependencies enforced; task solve rate 1.81%
one_line: DevAI: 55 realistic AI-app development tasks with 365 DAG-structured requirements, graded per-requirement by an agentic judge.
notes: Eval-methodology paper: classified the shipped artifact, DevAI + the Agent-as-a-Judge grader. Headline grading is the agentic judge checking per-task requirement checklists (rubric-judge); the human panel is the gold standard and LLM-as-a-Judge the baseline, hence grading_all. 55 tasks but 365 gradable requirements.
placement_reason: The contribution is a judging protocol for other systems' outputs: an agentic evaluator that reads workspaces and grades per-requirement DAG milestones, validated against a human panel (~90% alignment at ~2% cost), with DevAI shipped as the substrate for the judge. Born against SWE-bench's final-resolve-rate-only scoring and LLM-as-a-judge's blindness; explicitly named in the G3 family definition.

title: BigCodeBench: Benchmarking Code Generation with Diverse Function Calls and Complex Instructions
human_time: One task is a ~1,100-char spec with a ~10-line multi-library reference solution; the authors' own expert annotators solved sampled tasks blind - roughly 10-20 minutes each.
frontier_score: GPT-4o ~60% Pass@1 on Complete, <50% on Instruct (vs 97% human pass rate)
