one_line: 1,140 Python tasks composing 723 real library calls under complex specs, execution-graded by unit tests at 99% branch coverage.
notes: tool-use secondary is borderline: it tests composing library function calls inside generated code, not BFCL-style API emission. Provenance: GPT-4-synthesized from ODEX seeds under year-long human curation -> synthetic-generated. Contamination per explainer: 'private held-back test answers' + HF gating -> private-holdout. Saturation: ~60% quoted is 2024-dated and explainer notes frontier has since climbed -> closing.
placement_reason: Built to answer 'can a model read a rich spec and orchestrate many real library calls?' — 1,140 execution-graded Python tasks over 723 calls/139 libraries, headline being frontier skill (GPT-4o ~60% vs 97% human). The gap section frames a new construct (function-call composition + complex instructions) that HumanEval/MBPP never tested, not the same construct rebuilt for headroom, so A4 over B3.

title: τ-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
human_time: Minutes per conversation: a handful of dialogue turns plus a handful of tool calls per task; paper doesn't state a human baseline time.
frontier_score: gpt-4o function-calling agent: 48.2% average success (τ-retail/τ-airline); pass^8 reliability falls below 25%
one_line: A customer-service benchmark forcing agents to extract intent from a simulated human user, obey a written domain-policy document, and reproduce their own success across repeated trials — gpt-4o clears under half the tasks and its pass^8 reliability collapses below 25%.
notes: Introduces pass^k as a new reliability statistic (fraction of tasks solved on ALL of k independent trials) distinct from Codex's pass@k (at least one of k) — the reason this paper is dual-tagged benchmarks+evaluations.
placement_reason: Existing tool-use benchmarks (Gorilla, ToolLLM) hand the model a fully-specified task upfront; τ-bench instead makes information-gathering-from-a-human and written-policy compliance first-class parts of the tool-orchestration problem, closer to how service agents are actually deployed.

title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents
human_time: Human baseline 78.24% task success; paper doesn't report a per-task time.
frontier_score: GPT-4 + chain-of-thought: 14.41% task success vs. 78.24% human baseline
one_line: Four fully-hostable, real-world-scale websites (e-commerce, forum, GitLab, CMS) with 812 long-horizon tasks scored by functional correctness rather than reference-trajectory matching — GPT-4 with chain-of-thought closes only 14.41% of tasks.
notes: Foundational ancestor of the modern web-agent benchmark family (VisualWebArena, OSWorld, AndroidWorld, WebVoyager all extend its design).
placement_reason: The founding realistic, persistent-state web environment: agents act against fully-hostable clones of real sites and are scored by post-hoc functional-correctness checks on environment state, not by matching a reference action trajectory — the template every later web-agent benchmark in this family (VisualWebArena, OSWorld, AndroidWorld) reuses.

title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants
human_time: 92% human accuracy; paper doesn't report a per-task time.
frontier_score: GPT-4 + plugins: ~15% overall, 0% on the hardest (Level 3) tier
one_line: 466 human-crafted, human-validated questions that are conceptually trivial for people (92% human accuracy) but require an AI assistant to autonomously reason, browse the web, read diverse file types, and use tools to reach a single unambiguous factual answer.
notes: A single-question, single-final-answer interface (unlike WebArena/AgentBench's persistent stateful environments), even though the agent internally takes many tool-use steps — classified as single-turn on that basis.
placement_reason: Tests whether an assistant can autonomously orchestrate web search, code execution, and file reading to reach one unambiguous answer — a multi-tool general-assistant test in the same family as Gorilla/ToolLLM rather than a persistent-environment web-agent test.

title: RewardBench: Evaluating Reward Models for Language Modeling
human_time: Not applicable — the 'subject' being tested is a reward model, not a human or generalist LLM.
frontier_score: 80+ reward models scored; wide spread across chat/chat-hard/safety/reasoning subsets, no model near-ceiling on all four
one_line: A common benchmark and evaluation codebase for reward models — the classifiers and DPO checkpoints that decide what counts as a better answer during RLHF — exposing a wide, previously invisible gap between classifier-trained and DPO-derived reward models.
notes: Dual-tagged benchmarks+evaluations: it is both a named leaderboard (a survey of benchmarks would cite it) and the field's first standard evaluation codebase for an entire model class (reward models) that had none before.
placement_reason: Reward models are themselves graders (they decide which of two responses is better during RLHF); RewardBench is the benchmark that tests the graders, aggregating chat, reasoning, safety, and legacy preference subsets into one leaderboard — the reward-model peer of JudgeBench's LLM-judge testing.

title: JudgeBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-based Judges
human_time: Not applicable — the subject being tested is an LLM judge, not a human.
frontier_score: Vanilla-prompted GPT-4o: 50.86% (near-random); best reasoning-heavy judge (o3-mini-high): 80.86%
one_line: 350 objectively correct-vs-incorrect response pairs across knowledge, reasoning, math, and coding, sampled from a single strong model so pairs are style-matched — vanilla-prompted GPT-4o scores just 50.86% at telling right from wrong.
notes: Dual-tagged benchmarks+evaluations for the same reason as RewardBench: a named benchmark whose headline contribution is testing judges' factual/logical correctness rather than style-preference.
placement_reason: The LLM-judge peer of RewardBench: instead of testing whether a judge's verdict matches human style preference (MT-Bench, Arena-Hard), it tests whether the judge can tell objectively correct from incorrect at all — most vanilla judges can't beat chance.

title: FrontierMath: A Benchmark for Evaluating Advanced Mathematical Reasoning in AI
human_time: Research-mathematician-level problems; the paper reports professional mathematicians (some problem authors) needing hours to days per problem.
frontier_score: Every frontier LLM solved fewer than 2% of problems at publication
one_line: Hundreds of original, unpublished mathematics problems written and vetted by 60+ research mathematicians, checked by automated verification rather than human proof-grading — every frontier LLM solved fewer than 2% at publication.
notes: Kept as a private holdout by design, explicitly to prevent the contamination that saturated MATH/GSM8K; the direct successor once those benchmarks closed.
placement_reason: MATH and GPQA both closed within a couple of years of release; FrontierMath is the next summit — research-mathematician-authored problems held as a private holdout specifically so the wall can't be climbed by memorization.

title: Program Synthesis with Large Language Models
human_time: Short, one-sentence problems; not stated.
frontier_score: 137B-parameter model: 59.6% solve rate (80 samples); modern frontier models exceed 90%
one_line: A crowd-sourced benchmark of 974 short, one-sentence Python problems isolating natural-language-to-code synthesis from the narrative competitive-programming style of contemporaries like APPS and Codex/HumanEval.
notes: Alongside HumanEval, one of the two canonical basic-code-synthesis benchmarks; heavily memorized by modern LLMs, prompting EvalPlus's MBPP+ hardened-test successor.
placement_reason: HumanEval's sibling: the same natural-language-to-short-function synthesis task, built independently and crowd-sourced rather than hand-authored, giving the field two convergent basic code-synthesis benchmarks from the same year.

title: RE-Bench: Evaluating Frontier AI R&D Capabilities of Language Model Agents against Human Experts
human_time: 61 human ML experts across 71 runs, matched to 8-hour agent budgets, with some human runs extended to 32 hours
frontier_score: Agents score ~4x humans at a 2-hour budget, but plateau; humans reach ~2x the best agent's score by 32 hours
one_line: METR's benchmark pitting frontier AI agents against 61 real ML experts on 7 hand-crafted AI-research-engineering tasks under matched time budgets — agents win early but plateau while humans keep improving.
notes: Time-horizon plateau finding directly informs kwa-2025-time-horizons' cross-model trend analysis.
placement_reason: The human-anchored peer of MLAgentBench/MLE-bench: instead of scoring agents against a fixed leaderboard, it runs matched-budget agent-vs-expert trials on real ML R&D tasks to measure where agents' time-scaling curve diverges from humans'.

title: LiveBench: A Challenging, Contamination-Limited LLM Benchmark
human_time: Not stated.
frontier_score: o1-preview (top model at release): 64.7%
one_line: A monthly-refreshed, 18-task LLM benchmark spanning math, coding, reasoning, data analysis, instruction following, and language comprehension, scored entirely by objective ground truth rather than an LLM judge.
notes: Direct multi-domain generalization of LiveCodeBench's live-refresh, contamination-resistant design to six categories at once.
placement_reason: LiveCodeBench proved a monthly-refreshed, exam-derived question stream keeps a coding benchmark contamination-free; LiveBench is the same living-wall design generalized across six task categories at once.

title: ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs
human_time: Not stated.
frontier_score: Fine-tuned ToolLLaMA (7B) lands within a few points of ChatGPT on the paper's own ToolEval suite
one_line: An automated ChatGPT pipeline builds ToolBench — 126,486 instruction-and-solution-path traces over 16,464 real RapidAPI endpoints — plus a depth-first search decision tree (DFSDT) that finds working multi-tool solutions where ReAct gets stuck.
notes: ToolEval's own grading is ChatGPT-based pass/win-rate scoring, raising the same judge-trustworthiness question the paper's critique flags.
placement_reason: The large-scale-API peer of Gorilla: instead of one API family, orchestrates 16,000+ real RapidAPI endpoints via a synthetic ChatGPT-generated instruction/solution-path dataset and a search-based (DFSDT) planning method.

title: VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks
human_time: Human baseline 88.7% task success; paper doesn't report a per-task time.
frontier_score: Best VLM agent (GPT-4V + Set-of-Marks): 16.4% success vs. 88.7% human ceiling
one_line: A 910-task benchmark extending WebArena with genuinely visually-grounded web tasks across Classifieds, Shopping, and Reddit sites — the best VLM agent reaches 16.4% success against an 88.7% human ceiling.
notes: Direct extension of WebArena adding visual grounding as a first-class requirement, not just a text-accessibility-tree shortcut.
placement_reason: WebArena's visual-grounding sequel: the same persistent-website, functional-correctness design, but tasks are specifically constructed so a text-only accessibility-tree agent cannot solve them without seeing the page.

title: Evaluating Very Long-Term Conversational Memory of LLM Agents
human_time: A few minutes per question: given the ~9,200-token, ~300-turn, ~19-session transcript, locate the relevant turn(s) and answer one recall / temporal-reasoning question - humans reach 87.9 QA-F1 this way.
frontier_score: GPT-4-turbo 32.1 QA-F1 vs 87.9 human (best of the models tested; worst on temporal / multi-hop recall)
one_line: 50 very-long-term dialogues (avg 300 turns, 19 sessions, spanning months) with 7,512 questions plus event-summary and multimodal-generation tasks, probing cross-session memory.
notes: Provenance is machine-human: two persona- and temporal-event-graph-conditioned LLM agents generate each dialogue, then annotators edit ~15% for long-range consistency. grading_primary is the QA task's word-overlap F1 (the 32.1-vs-87.9 headline); the event-summarization and multimodal-dialogue-generation tasks add ROUGE / MMRelevance-style proxy metrics. Images are inputs, not tools (tool_access none).
placement_reason: Headline is a capability verdict - even GPT-4-turbo answers very-long-term recall/temporal-reasoning questions at 32.1 F1 against 87.9 human, so cross-session conversational memory is unsolved. A multi-turn conversation-ability probe (converse and remember across dozens of sessions), i.e. A7 'communication' extended to a months-long horizon, not a grader audit (B) or a durability wall (C).

title: Beyond Goldfish Memory: Long-Term Open-Domain Conversation
human_time: A few minutes: read the up-to-5-session (~1,600-token) history and produce or judge one context-appropriate next utterance.
frontier_score: Retrieval- and summary-memory-augmented BST 2.7B beat plain encoder-decoder fine-tuning on validation perplexity and human preference (no single accuracy ceiling - perplexity/preference-graded)
one_line: First public multi-session dialogue-memory dataset: crowdsourced human-human chats over up to 5 sessions (hours-to-days apart) with per-turn persona summaries.
notes: Dataset-plus-modeling paper: it introduces the Multi-Session Chat crowdsourcing protocol (~5,001 collected episodes; session 1 is a PersonaChat conversation, personas from Zhang et al. 2018) and the retrieval-/summary-memory models built on it. Graded by validation perplexity (proxy) and human preference, with no fixed accuracy ceiling, so saturation is not-applicable and items_count is null. Effectively superseded as a memory probe by LoCoMo (2024), which is ~9x longer.
placement_reason: First public probe of whether a dialogue model can hold a coherent conversation across multiple sessions using earlier context - a conversation-ability question (A7 'communication'), not an audit of a grader or a frontier wall. Its retrieval-/summary-memory models are the paper's proposed fix to that capability gap, not the object being classified.
